name: Basil Ground Beef Spaghetti (Upgraded)
ingredients:
2-3 lb ground beef (80/20)
1 white onion, finely diced
1 medium carrot, finely diced
1 celery stalk, finely diced
1 red bell pepper, diced
12 crimini mushrooms, chopped
2 tbsp tomato paste
1/2 cup dry red wine (Chianti, Sangiovese, or similar)
1 jar (24 oz) tomato-based pasta sauce (garlic tomato, arrabbiata, or truffle marinara)
1 bunch fresh basil, chopped (reserve half for garnish)
1 lb spaghetti
2 tbsp olive oil
1 tbsp butter
Salt and black pepper to taste
Red pepper flakes to taste
Grated parmesan to taste

Optional upgrades:
2-3 anchovy fillets
1 parmesan rind
1 tsp Worcestershire or balsamic vinegar
1/4 cup whole milk or heavy cream
Finishing olive oil, for drizzling
directions:
Build the soffritto: Heat olive oil and butter in a large Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the diced onion, carrot, and celery with a pinch of salt. Sweat gently for 10-12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened and jammy — do not brown. This is the flavor foundation; don't rush it.
Increase heat to medium-high. Push the soffritto to one side and add the ground beef in a single layer. Season generously with salt and pepper. Let it sear undisturbed for 3-4 minutes to develop a browned crust, then break it up and continue cooking 4-5 minutes more until no pink remains. Do not drain the fat unless truly excessive.
(Optional) Melt in 2-3 anchovy fillets now — they'll disappear into the sauce and add deep savory backbone.
Stir in the tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens from bright red to a rusty brick color. This caramelizes the sugars and kills the raw tomato taste.
Add red pepper flakes. Pour in the red wine and scrape up any browned bits from the bottom of the pan. Let it bubble and reduce until nearly dry, about 3-4 minutes.
Add the diced bell pepper and mushrooms. Cook 5-6 minutes until the mushrooms release their moisture and begin to brown.
Pour in the jar of sauce. (Optional) Add a parmesan rind and a splash of Worcestershire or balsamic. Reduce heat to low and simmer uncovered for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Meanwhile, bring a large pot of well-salted water to a boil. Cook the spaghetti 1 minute shy of al dente. Reserve 1 cup of pasta water before draining.
(Optional) Stir 1/4 cup whole milk or cream into the sauce for a rounder, silkier finish.
Remove the parmesan rind if used. Stir half the chopped basil into the sauce.
Transfer the drained spaghetti directly into the sauce along with a splash of pasta water. Toss vigorously over medium heat for 60-90 seconds until the pasta is coated and the sauce clings — add more pasta water as needed to loosen.
Serve immediately, topped with remaining fresh basil, a generous shower of microplaned parmesan, and a drizzle of good olive oil if desired.
notes: The soffritto (onion + carrot + celery sweated slowly) and the tomato paste caramelization are the two biggest upgrades over the original — don't skip them. The red wine deglaze is the third. Everything listed as optional stacks additional depth: anchovies add savory backbone without any fishiness, the parmesan rind enriches the simmer, and a splash of cream rounds out acidity for a ragù-like finish. Finishing the pasta directly in the sauce with a splash of pasta water (rather than plating sauce over pasta) is what makes each strand actually taste like the sauce instead of just wearing it.
